title: Should a place record the way it sounds before it changes?
summary: Argue whether the soundscape of a neighbourhood — its calls, bells, and machines — deserves the same effort of preservation we give to its buildings.
topic: Whether the disappearing sound of a place is worth recording and preserving before redevelopment erases it
prompt: Write a 450–600-word argumentative essay on whether a neighbourhood should record its own soundscape — the vendors' calls, the trams, the call to prayer, the workshops — before redevelopment changes it for good. Argue why sound is harder to protect than buildings, and whether an archive of vanished sound is preservation or merely a souvenir of loss. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and concession markers.
hintStarters: What disappears first when a neighbourhood is rebuilt is not its walls but…
Granted, a recording cannot bring back the street that produced it, and yet…
It is sound, more than any façade, that tells you whether a place is still…
We protect what we can photograph, and quietly let go of everything that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cảnh quan âm thanh / không gian âm thanh
tái phát triển / quy hoạch lại
tiếng ồn nền / âm thanh môi trường xung quanh
tiếng rao của người bán hàng
quá trình tư sản hóa khu phố
kết cấu / chất riêng của một nơi
tuyên bố quyền / khẳng định quyền với
làm vô trùng / làm mất sức sống

sampleText:
Before the developers reach Cairo's Bulaq, the photographers come. They document the carved doors and the leaning balconies, the things a camera can hold. What no one is sent to record is the noise the quarter makes — the metalworkers' hammering that starts before dawn, the bread-seller's two-note cry, the call to prayer arriving slightly out of step from four nearby minarets. When the bulldozers finish, the doors may survive in a museum. The sound will simply be gone, and no one will be able to say exactly when it stopped.

What disappears first when a neighbourhood is rebuilt is not its walls but its noise, and it disappears without anyone deciding to remove it. A building can be listed, fenced, and argued over in a courtroom; a sound cannot be fenced. It is sound, more than any façade, that tells you whether a place is still itself or has been hollowed into a backdrop. A street that has lost its workshops looks much the same in a photograph and is unrecognisable to the ear. This is why a serious case can be made for recording a place before it changes — not the buildings, which are already protected, but the soundscape, which is protected by no one.

Consider the trams of old Lisbon, whose rattle and bell were recorded by a sound archivist in the 1990s, years before the lines were rerouted for tourists; those recordings are now the only evidence that the city once sounded the way the elderly remember it. Or consider a market in Hanoi where a researcher captured the dawn calls of the last itinerant vendors before the stalls were moved indoors. The market still exists. The sound it made does not, except on a hard drive.

Granted, a recording cannot bring back the street that produced it, and there is something melancholy, even dishonest, in a file that lets us hear a neighbourhood we did our nothing to save. An archive of vanished sound can become an alibi: proof that we noticed the loss without lifting a finger to prevent it. To record a place as it dies is not the same as keeping it alive, and it would be a lie to suggest otherwise. The objection cuts deep.

Even so, the choice is rarely between recording a place and saving it; more often it is between recording it and losing it without trace. We protect what we can photograph, and quietly let go of everything that cannot be framed — which means the most living part of a place, its sound, is the part we are least equipped to keep. A recording will not stop the bulldozers. What it can do is refuse the second erasure, the one that follows the first: the slow forgetting in which people come to believe the street was always this quiet, that nothing was lost because nothing was ever heard.

A neighbourhood that records its own sound is not indulging in nostalgia. It is leaving testimony — bearing witness to a way of living that was loud, particular, and real, so that the silence which replaces it can at least be recognised as a silence, and not mistaken for peace.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to argue sound matters more than surface
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is sound, more than any façade, that tells you whether a place is still itself' dùng câu chẻ it-cleft để nhấn mạnh âm thanh là yếu tố quyết định, đẩy lùi 'mặt tiền' xuống hàng thứ yếu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is sound, more than any façade, that tells you whether a place is still itself' uses an it-cleft to foreground sound as the decisive marker, demoting the visible façade.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to show sound vanishing with no agent
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'it disappears without anyone deciding to remove it' và 'whose rattle and bell were recorded' dùng cấu trúc khiến âm thanh biến mất như một sự kiện không có chủ thể, làm nổi bật rằng không ai chịu trách nhiệm bảo vệ nó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'it disappears without anyone deciding to remove it' and 'whose rattle and bell were recorded' use agentless framing and the passive to show sound vanishing with no one responsible for protecting it.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trước khi các nhà phát triển tiến đến khu Bulaq của Cairo, những nhiếp ảnh gia đến trước. Họ ghi lại những cánh cửa chạm khắc và những ban công nghiêng ngả, những thứ mà một chiếc máy ảnh có thể giữ lại. Cái không ai được phái đến để ghi lại là tiếng động mà khu này tạo ra — tiếng búa của những người thợ kim loại bắt đầu trước bình minh, tiếng rao hai nốt của người bán bánh mì, lời gọi cầu nguyện vọng đến hơi lệch nhịp từ bốn tháp giáo đường gần đó. Khi những chiếc máy ủi làm xong, những cánh cửa có thể sống sót trong một bảo tàng. Âm thanh thì đơn giản là sẽ biến mất, và không ai có thể nói chính xác nó đã ngừng vào lúc nào.

Cái biến mất đầu tiên khi một khu phố được xây lại không phải là những bức tường mà là tiếng động của nó, và nó biến mất mà không ai quyết định gỡ bỏ nó. Một tòa nhà có thể được xếp hạng, rào lại, và tranh cãi trước tòa; một âm thanh thì không thể bị rào lại. Chính âm thanh, hơn bất kỳ mặt tiền nào, mới cho bạn biết một nơi có còn là chính nó hay đã bị rỗng hóa thành một phông nền. Một con phố đã mất đi những xưởng thợ trông gần như y hệt trong một bức ảnh và lại không thể nhận ra được bằng tai. Đây là lý do một lập luận nghiêm túc có thể được đưa ra cho việc ghi lại một nơi trước khi nó thay đổi — không phải những tòa nhà, vốn đã được bảo vệ, mà là không gian âm thanh, thứ không được ai bảo vệ.

Hãy xét đến những chiếc tàu điện của Lisbon xưa, mà tiếng lạch cạch và tiếng chuông của chúng đã được một người lưu trữ âm thanh ghi lại vào những năm 1990, nhiều năm trước khi các tuyến được đổi hướng phục vụ du khách; những bản thu đó nay là bằng chứng duy nhất rằng thành phố từng vang lên đúng như cách những người già nhớ về nó. Hoặc hãy xét đến một khu chợ ở Hà Nội nơi một nhà nghiên cứu đã ghi lại những tiếng rao lúc bình minh của những người bán hàng rong cuối cùng trước khi các quầy được chuyển vào trong nhà. Khu chợ vẫn còn đó. Âm thanh mà nó tạo ra thì không, ngoại trừ trên một ổ cứng.

Công nhận, một bản thu âm không thể đem lại con phố đã tạo ra nó, và có điều gì đó buồn bã, thậm chí thiếu trung thực, trong một tập tin cho phép ta nghe lại một khu phố mà ta đã chẳng làm gì để cứu. Một kho lưu trữ âm thanh đã biến mất có thể trở thành một cái cớ ngoại phạm: bằng chứng rằng ta đã để ý đến sự mất mát mà không nhấc một ngón tay để ngăn nó lại. Ghi lại một nơi khi nó đang chết đi thì không giống với việc giữ cho nó sống, và sẽ là dối trá nếu gợi ý điều ngược lại. Phản đối này đâm sâu.

Dẫu vậy, lựa chọn hiếm khi là giữa ghi lại một nơi và cứu nó; thường hơn là giữa ghi lại nó và đánh mất nó không để lại dấu vết. Chúng ta bảo vệ những gì ta có thể chụp ảnh, và lặng lẽ buông bỏ tất cả những gì không thể đóng khung lại — nghĩa là phần sống động nhất của một nơi, âm thanh của nó, lại là phần ta được trang bị kém nhất để giữ lại. Một bản thu âm sẽ không ngăn được những chiếc máy ủi. Điều nó có thể làm là từ chối sự xóa bỏ thứ hai, sự xóa bỏ đến sau cái thứ nhất: sự lãng quên chậm rãi trong đó người ta dần tin rằng con phố vốn luôn yên ắng như thế, rằng chẳng có gì mất đi vì chẳng có gì từng được nghe thấy.

Một khu phố ghi lại âm thanh của chính mình không phải đang đắm chìm trong hoài niệm. Nó đang để lại lời chứng — làm chứng cho một lối sống từng ồn ào, riêng biệt, và có thật, để cho sự im lặng thay thế nó ít nhất cũng có thể được nhận ra là một sự im lặng, chứ không bị nhầm là sự yên bình.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to argue that sound matters more than appearance?
options: Sound tells you it is more than any façade a place still itself whether.
Whether a place is still itself it is the façade more than sound tells you.
More than any façade a place still itself sound it is tells whether you.
It is sound, more than any façade, that tells you whether a place is still itself.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X … that …' is an it-cleft foregrounding sound over the visible façade.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is sound … that tells you …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to show the soundscape vanishing with no responsible agent?
options: It disappears without anyone deciding to remove it.
The council deliberately switched off all the neighbourhood's sounds.
Someone clearly chose to delete the sound on purpose one day.
Remove it deciding anyone without disappears it the sound.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'without anyone deciding to remove it' frames the loss as agentless, which is the point.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option says the sound vanishes though no one removed it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the counter-argument most fairly?
options: Obviously only sentimental fools care about old street noises.
Granted, a recording cannot bring back the street that produced it.
Of course recordings are completely pointless and always have been.
Naturally, anyone who records a place is just showing off.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' honestly admits the recording's central limitation.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits a real weakness in the writer's own case?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'intangible' mean as applied to a soundscape?
options: Loud and overwhelming
Officially protected by law
Unable to be touched or physically held
Recorded on a hard drive
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Intangible' means it has no physical form, which is why it cannot be 'fenced'.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something with no physical body.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the concession back to the argument?
options: In conclusion, recordings achieve absolutely nothing.
On the contrary, buildings are the only thing that matters.
Anyway, sound is not important to anyone really.
Even so, the choice is rarely between recording a place and saving it; more often it is between recording it and losing it without trace.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the concession and reframes the real choice the essay defends.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase grants the prior point and then redefines the choice?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent argumentative register?
options: A building can be listed, fenced, and argued over in a courtroom; a sound cannot be fenced.
Honestly it's just so depressing how everywhere sounds the same now.
Basically old places were loud and new places are boring, end of story.
It kind of bums me out that nobody records this stuff, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and analytical; the others slip into casual speech.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', and 'kind of bums me out'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why, according to the essay, is sound harder to protect than buildings?
options: Because sound is too loud to record accurately
Because a building can be listed and fenced, but a sound cannot be fenced
Because buildings are worth more money
Because no one cares about buildings either
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay contrasts the legal protectability of buildings with the unfenceable nature of sound.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: A building can be listing, to fence, and argued in a courtroom over.
A building can be listed, the fencing, and to argue over a courtroom.
A building can be listed, fenced, and argued over in a courtroom.
A building listing can be, fenced over, and a courtroom to argue.
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched past-participle phrases ('listed, fenced, argued over') form a clean parallel list.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same passive form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the 'second erasure' the essay warns against?
options: The demolition of the buildings
The deletion of the recording itself
The rerouting of the trams
The slow forgetting in which people believe the street was always this quiet
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'second erasure' is the forgetting that follows the physical loss.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the line about believing 'nothing was lost'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to bear witness to' mean?
options: To testify to or formally acknowledge that something was real
To prevent something from happening
To photograph something
To profit from something
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To bear witness to' means to attest that something existed or occurred.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of giving testimony that something was real.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a semicolon to join two related independent clauses?
options: The market still exists, the sound; it made does not.
The market still exists; the sound it made does not.
The market; still exists the sound it made does not.
The market still; exists the sound it made; does not.
mcQuestions.explanation: A semicolon correctly links two complete, closely related clauses.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places the semicolon between two full sentences?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's final claim about recording a neighbourhood's sound?
options: It is pure nostalgia and should be avoided
It guarantees the neighbourhood will be saved
It is leaving testimony so the replacing silence is recognised as loss, not mistaken for peace
It is more important than saving the buildings
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay reframes recording as testimony against being forgotten, not as rescue.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the closing distinction between silence and peace.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does an 'alibi' refer to in this essay?
options: A legal document protecting a building
A type of sound recording device
A souvenir bought by tourists
An excuse that lets us feel we noticed a loss without acting to prevent it
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay warns the archive can become an 'alibi' — proof of noticing without acting.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of evidence we cared, used to excuse inaction.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's honest concession?
options: To record a place as it dies is not the same as keeping it alive.
Recording a dying place saves it completely.
A recording is always better than the real place.
No one should ever bother recording anything.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the limit of recording honestly, strengthening the later argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits recording is not the same as rescuing?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option avoids overstating the power of a recording?
options: A recording will single-handedly stop redevelopment everywhere.
A recording will not stop the bulldozers; what it can do is refuse the second erasure.
A recording brings any demolished street completely back to life.
Recording a place makes redevelopment illegal.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states clearly what a recording cannot and can do, keeping the claim honest.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the recording's limit while naming its real value?

options: Easy to copy or replicate
Officially listed as heritage
Impossible to substitute once lost
Cheap and widely available
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Irreplaceable' means nothing else can take its place once it is gone.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something that cannot be replaced.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a place you knew as a child. What did it sound like, and does it still sound that way? If a recording of its lost sounds existed, would hearing it bring the place back to you — or would it only make the silence that replaced it harder to ignore?
